summary: Matched MSL/abort_exit.c by replacing address-placeholder globals with MSL-style names, making the atexit and exit-state storage static, iterating the _dtors table directly from C, and marking abort_exit.c as Matching in configure.py. The PR also removed the small hand-authored abort_exit .dtors asm file and a 506-line Runtime/runtime.s asm fallback, and adjusted GALE01 symbols/splits for the renamed and resized MSL abort/exit data.

key_files.reason: Main matching source change: renamed placeholder variables, used static MSL abort/exit state, switched destructor traversal to _dtors, and kept exit shutdown ordering.
Replaced MSL_AbortExit_* placeholder symbols with atexit_funcs, __atexit_funcs, __aborting, atexit_curr_func, __atexit_curr_func, __stdio_exit, and __console_exit.
Deleted the hand-written .dtors table containing __destroy_global_chain, __fini_cpp_exceptions, __destroy_global_chain, and NULL.
Deleted a 506-line asm fallback containing compiler/runtime helper routines such as __cvt_fp2unsigned, __div2u, __div2i, __mod2u, __mod2i, __shl2i, __shr2u, __shr2i, __cvt_sll_flt, and __constants.

what_changed: abort_exit.c was promoted from NonMatching to Matching in configure.py.

The abort_exit data symbols were renamed from address-based placeholders like MSL_AbortExit_804A2F48 and MSL_AbortExit_804D7060 to semantic names: atexit_funcs, __atexit_funcs, __aborting, atexit_curr_func, __atexit_curr_func, __stdio_exit, and __console_exit.
The atexit arrays and abort/exit state in abort_exit.c were made static rather than exposed as generic global placeholder variables.

exit() now tests __aborting, drains atexit_funcs with atexit_curr_func, calls __destroy_global_chain(), walks _dtors until NULL, optionally calls and clears __stdio_exit, drains __atexit_funcs, kills critical regions, optionally calls and clears __console_exit, then calls _ExitProcess().
The previous for-loop over MSL_AbortExit_803B7260 was rewritten as explicit pointer initialization to _dtors followed by a while (*dtor != NULL) loop.
asm/MSL/abort_exit.s was deleted, removing the local .dtors table definition that had been named MSL_AbortExit_803B7260.
config/GALE01/symbols.txt was updated to reflect the new semantic symbol names for the abort_exit .bss and .sbss data.
config/GALE01/splits.txt shrank the abort_exit .sbss range by 4 bytes, ending at 0x804D7074 instead of 0x804D7078.
asm/Runtime/runtime.s was removed entirely, eliminating an assembly fallback for several Runtime helper functions and constants.
smart_moves: Replacing address-derived placeholder names with plausible MSL runtime names made the source self-documenting and aligned it with known abort/exit library concepts.
Using static storage for abort_exit internals matched the intended C ownership better than keeping decomp placeholder globals in the source.
Walking the _dtors sentinel table directly in C avoided carrying a local assembly file just to represent the destructor list.
The explicit while-loop over _dtors preserved the generated control-flow shape while still expressing the logic in idiomatic C.
The PR paired the C match with all required project bookkeeping: symbols.txt renames, splits.txt size adjustment, configure.py status change, and asm file deletion.
decomp_lessons: For MSL runtime files, address-placeholder globals are often match blockers; converting them to known library names and correct static/global linkage can unlock a clean match.
Destructor tables should be modeled via the project’s canonical symbol when possible, here _dtors, rather than maintaining a bespoke asm symbol such as MSL_AbortExit_803B7260.

When matching exit/abort code, preserve shutdown ordering carefully: atexit callbacks, global destructor chain, .dtors traversal, stdio cleanup, __atexit callbacks, critical-region teardown, console cleanup, then process exit.
A successful C match should remove obsolete asm fallbacks and update configure.py immediately so future builds no longer depend on the assembly source.
Small .sbss range changes can accompany static data cleanup; verify splits.txt after renaming or removing abort/exit state variables.
naming_conventions: Use semantic MSL names instead of MSL_AbortExit_ADDRESS placeholders for recovered library globals.
Plain atexit callback storage used names atexit_funcs and atexit_curr_func.
Double-underscore MSL/internal exit storage used names __atexit_funcs and __atexit_curr_func.
Exit hook function pointers were named __stdio_exit and __console_exit.
The destructor table was referenced as _dtors rather than an address-based MSL_AbortExit_803B7260 symbol.
Known runtime/library entry points retained double-underscore names such as __destroy_global_chain, __kill_critical_regions, and __fini_cpp_exceptions.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Deleted asm/MSL/abort_exit.s after replacing the local .dtors table with an extern _dtors reference from C.
Rewrote the destructor traversal from a for-loop over a placeholder symbol into an explicit dtor assignment plus while-loop, likely to match compiler codegen more closely.
Changed data declarations to static arrays and counters to better match storage/linkage and emitted symbol layout.
Removed asm/Runtime/runtime.s, including assembly implementations of 64-bit arithmetic/shift helpers and conversion helpers, as part of reducing remaining handwritten runtime assembly.
Used configure.py’s Object(Matching, ...) switch as the final declaration that the source now matches.
follow_up_queries: Find other MSL files still using MSL_*_ADDRESS placeholder globals that could be renamed to known library symbols.
Search for remaining asm files that only define tables or constants and can be replaced by canonical extern symbols in C.
Audit exit/abort related code for correct ordering around _dtors, __destroy_global_chain, __stdio_exit, and __console_exit.
Check whether other Runtime helper asm fallbacks can be removed after their C counterparts are marked matching.
